questions.question: What is the first step to start journaling in Notion?

options.text: Create a database for all your journal entries
options.explanation: Notion recommends beginning with a database to store every entry in one place.

options.text: Write a blog post about journaling
options.explanation: A blog post is unrelated to setting up a personal journal in Notion.

options.text: Install a third‑party journaling plugin
options.explanation: Notion’s built‑in database features are sufficient; no plugin is required.

options.text: Share a public page with friends
options.explanation: Sharing comes after you have created your journal, not before.

questions.question: Which types of content can you embed inside a Notion journal page?

options.text: Plain text only
options.explanation: Notion supports more than just text.

options.text: Text, images, and videos
options.explanation: The guide states you can add text, images, or even videos inside journal pages.

options.text: Only PDF documents
options.explanation: PDFs can be added, but they are not the only supported format.

options.text: Only spreadsheets
options.explanation: Spreadsheets are a type of block, but not the only one you can embed.

questions.question: How can you organize your journal entries by date in Notion?

options.text: Manually rename each page with the date
options.explanation: Renaming is tedious and doesn’t allow automatic sorting.

options.text: Add a date property to the database and sort by it
options.explanation: A date property lets you sort entries chronologically with a single click.

options.text: Create a separate database for each month
options.explanation: That would fragment your journal and make searching harder.

options.text: Use tags only, without a date field
options.explanation: Tags help categorize, but they don’t provide chronological ordering.

questions.question: Which feature lets you view only journal entries that have a specific tag, such as "travel"?

options.text: Sorting
options.explanation: Sorting changes order but does not hide entries.

options.text: Filtering
options.explanation: Filters allow you to display only rows that meet certain criteria, like a tag.

options.text: Exporting
options.explanation: Exporting creates a file; it doesn’t change what you see inside Notion.

options.text: Duplicating
options.explanation: Duplicating creates a copy; it doesn’t limit the view.

questions.question: If you decide you prefer a regular page over a database entry for a particular journal entry, what can you do?

options.text: Delete the entry and start over
options.explanation: You don’t need to delete; Notion lets you convert the entry.

options.text: Convert the database entry into a regular page
options.explanation: Notion allows you to change an entry from a database row to a standalone page.

options.text: Leave it as a database entry; it cannot be changed
options.explanation: The guide explicitly says you can switch to a regular page later.

options.text: Create a new workspace for that entry
options.explanation: A new workspace is unnecessary for a single entry.

questions.question: Where can you find pre‑made journal templates within Notion?

options.text: In the Help Center articles
options.explanation: Help articles explain features but don’t host templates.

options.text: In the Template Gallery
options.explanation: The guide points users to browse over 10,000 templates in the template gallery.

options.text: In the Settings & Members menu
options.explanation: Settings manage account preferences, not template browsing.

options.text: On the official YouTube channel
options.explanation: YouTube videos may showcase templates, but the gallery is the direct source.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about sorting journal entries is FALSE?

options.text: You can sort entries from most recent to oldest
options.explanation: Sorting by date from newest to oldest is a supported feature.

options.text: Sorting can be based on any property, such as tags
options.explanation: Notion lets you sort by any column/property you create.

options.text: Sorting automatically deletes older entries
options.explanation: Sorting only changes display order; it never removes data.

options.text: Sorting changes the order displayed in the database view
options.explanation: This accurately describes what sorting does.

questions.question: How does Notion’s drag‑and‑drop functionality affect the structure of a journal page?

options.text: It permanently locks content in place
options.explanation: Blocks remain editable after being moved.

options.text: It allows you to rearrange blocks while still being able to edit them later
options.explanation: Drag‑and‑drop reorders blocks, and you can still modify any block afterward.

options.text: It creates duplicate copies of the moved content
options.explanation: Moving does not duplicate; it simply changes position.

options.text: It converts blocks into database entries
options.explanation: Blocks stay as blocks; they don’t become database rows by dragging.
